denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter concerns the request your provider submitted for surveillance computed tomography (CT) of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis every 3 months following treatment for colon cancer. After review under the applicable Cigna Medical Coverage Policy and Milliman Care Guidelines (MCG), the request is denied as not medically necessary at the requested frequency. Determination date: May 26, 2026.

Cigna's guidelines provide that post-treatment surveillance imaging for colorectal cancer is appropriate at an interval of every 6 to 12 months. The requested cadence of every 3 months exceeds that interval, and the submitted documentation does not establish that imaging at this higher frequency is medically necessary. The surveillance criteria applied in this review reflect colorectal cancer outcomes data in patients ages 65 and older.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the specific medical necessity criteria and the documents relied upon in reaching this decision. If your provider wishes to discuss this determination, a peer-to-peer review with a Cigna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal. To file an internal appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter, along with any additional clinical information you would like considered. You may also request an external review by an independent review organization after the internal appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Coverage Review Department
clinical_context: The patient is a 38-year-old man with stage III (T3N1, 2 of 18 nodes positive) colon adenocarcinoma diagnosed at age 37, status post laparoscopic colectomy and twelve cycles of adjuvant FOLFOX completed in late 2025; tumor testing showed microsatellite-stable disease. He is now in post-treatment surveillance. His carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) has risen from 2.1 ng/mL to 4.8 ng/mL over two consecutive measurements in the first half of 2026, a trend concerning for recurrence, although cross-sectional imaging to date has been negative. His oncologist requests CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis every 3 months because young-onset colorectal cancer (diagnosis before age 50) is associated with more aggressive biology and higher early-recurrence rates, for which intensified surveillance is clinically supported. The every-6-to-12-month cadence the insurer cites is drawn from outcomes data in older patients (ages 65 and older) and does not reflect the recurrence risk of a 38-year-old with node-positive, microsatellite-stable disease and a rising tumor marker. National guidelines support more frequent imaging in higher-risk survivors, and the rising CEA further supports the requested interval.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Cigna, Medical Necessity, oncology, 26-40, M, frequency_excessive). Diagnosis is oncology; surveillance CT realistic. Rationale enacts frequency_excessive (every-3-month exceeds guideline). Age band not 71+, null employer_archetype fine.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. Young-onset stage III colon cancer post-resection on surveillance with FOLFOX is coherent and current. Insurers routinely deny every-3-month CT citing generic cadence; the ages-65+ criteria mismatch is an authentic misapplied-guideline the context rebuts. Cigna Medical Coverage Policy + MCG matches real self-funded behavior; missing cost-liability is a plausible omission.

38-year-old man with young-onset stage III (T3N1) colon adenocarcinoma status post colectomy and adjuvant FOLFOX, now in surveillance with a documented rising CEA trend

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Real guideline cited but exploitably applied: criteria anchored to ages-65+ data applied to a 38yo with young-onset, node-positive, MSS disease; a documented rising CEA (2.1 to 4.8) is an objective recurrence signal supporting intensified imaging. Procedurally clean, so the appeal is a clinical/factual mismatch — strong but not guaranteed since the guideline genuinely exists.
